sentences.text: Yeah.
Now, it was very hard winter time, because you could see that somebody-- that-- that-- that somebody is coming there.
You know, that-- you-- you see the tracks like-- like a horse and-- and a sleigh, and somebody went down.
So every day, I was [INAUDIBLE] like the manager.
Every day early-- early in the morning, I had to go out with a-- with a burlap bag, and I try to-- to mask the-- the snow, like all the-- all the-- how you call them-- The tracks?
--the tracks.
To wipe out all the tracks.
Then I told to my brother, you know what would be good?
Maybe if you bring somewhere a pair of skis.
And I would ski around here with skis.
They-- nobody would think the Jews are skiing, or-- Jews do not ski.
They-- they were-- maybe this would-- He brought me a pair of skis.
And I went after the snow, when they came in, brought in the stuff, I used to try to wipe.
And I put on my skis and I was driving around.
I ski there.
But this didn't help.
We were there from November, 1942, till they discovered us by the end of March, 1943.
Was about 20 people in that time.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We-- we-- [INAUDIBLE] we even bought ourselves a little pig.
It's not kosher.
[LAUGHS]
You see, what it was, where we-- in the grotto, we changed the-- at night-- during the day, we slept.
At night we were up.
We changed day for night.
All day, we were sleeping.
And at night, we-- we were cooking, and this is-- it was, you know, when I just was thinking, in hey, we are not in the ghetto.
It's good.
Well, the-- the only news that-- my brother come.
And tell that they made a-- they kill people here, and they kill over-- this was the news.
They kill.
They got-- this was killed and this was killed and this was killed.
This was the only news that we had at that time.
So this was going on like that through the winter.
But we-- we needed another exit because we-- what happen if they going to stay there.
Is-- they'll put somebody there, and we'll die from hunger.
We have to have another exit.
But a cave-- I'll explain the way a cave is inside.
A cave is everything stone.
All the walls.
Everything is stone.
Well, this was different places.
Some places was high, like about 10 feet.
Some places were very low.
This was not a cave like the second one, which I'm going to tell you.
So we-- we-- everything stone.
And not-- only where you walk is soft.
So we were looking around.
How can we made an exit if everything is stone?
How can we do this?
But we were walking around and crawling in places.
There was not cleaned out, the-- the cave.
Wild.
We were lying down on the stomach and crawling, let's say, 50 feet under the stone.
And we came on a place that was higher.
As we-- in one place, we come and we see a little pile of dirt about four feet high just like that.
It means it came from somewhere.
We look up, and we see between two stones there's an opening.
I would say no more than 18 inches.
And we tried with the shovel and see hey, it's soft.
It's good earth.
But it's narrow.
But my brother was very strong.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] He took a chisel and then a-- and-- and enlarged this opening.
No.
It's a good question.
I don't say this here.
We had little-- little lamp-- little bottles.
A little bottle with a little-- put in kerosene, and a little wick like a candle.
And this gave more light than all those lights here.
Because--
It's not a lantern.
It's just a little glass, a little-- little bottle.
You put in kerosene.
And in there neck there you put in a little wick.
You-- and it was giving a lot of light because it's so dark there.
If you put a finger in front of you, you don't see nothing.
This was our-- they call it [POLISH].
Like a Polish word.
This was our-- our light.
There-- if you-- if you didn't have matches with you, and the light, you-- in-- you could stay there forever.
Finished.
So we find this place, and we start digging upwards.
We put in a burlap bag on the head.
And we see that the ground is very good, and we start digging upwards.
We-- and we made about three-- 36 inches.
About three feet.
Around like a big barrel.
And we upwards.
And in the left side of the wall, we made a place where to rest our feet, a little step.
Dug out deep, about six inches deep.
Put a little piece of wood inside, like made a step.
Step here, step here.
And up, up.
My brother used to come at night.
He could come from the city.
He worked all night.
Me and him, we were digging.
And this was about four or five stories high.
We made like a big chimney.
Very, very high.
We were really lucky.
Today, I understand because I'm in construction.
That we were lucky that we didn't find some stones on this here.
Very good ground.
And we made this maybe three or four stories high.
Because 20 people went inside.
There must be about four, five stories high.
Now, when we made this year, we open it, and we came out on a field.
Far away from our cave.
So we had to-- and we see that this is in a place that farmers gonna work in the spring.
We had to-- we had to-- how you call it-- to-- they-- to mask it.
They shouldn't see it.
So what I did, we put a piece of wood about-- let's say about seven, eight feet long.
A lot.
About two feet deep.
And we went down to the village.
And we borrow-- or we stole, let's say.
Let's say we stole it.
Because we had those wells, water to pull up.
I don't know if you-- a big chain, maybe 50 feet.
Big chain.
We tie down the chain in the middle so when you walk up, you hold on and you walk up on this here.
And then I made a little frame like-- I didn't have no nails, but I made it like that, a frame.
About I would say 36 inches, a frame round.
Not round, square.
And then also I put-- I didn't have no plywood there.
Put a few pieces of board together.
Some.
And we put this on the top.
And we covered it up with earth about two feet high.
The farmer could plow and he couldn't find it.
And we have this.
This was a-- this was a-- a big success.
Big thing for us.
And in this place where-- where we-- we were in a different place.
But while this chimney was here, we all had to move there.
But this was very low.
So we dug trenches, just to walk.
And we made places.
This family, we made bunks there, beds to sleep here.
He slept there.
My sister here.
My aunt here.
We all set up there.
Near-- near this-- near this place.
Because if anything happened, we should be close to run away.
I would say about two months.
So we-- all the families, we had moved from this place, another place, into the new place.
The [POLISH] were one family in one place.
The [POLISH].
The Stermers, another place.
My sister, Henia, my [POLISH] with the kids.
And Nissen used to bring the food.
And one day, we were sleeping during the day.
We had also a family from our time.
His name is [POLISH] with two kids.
The kids are-- one, the daughter is in Montreal.
They were also in that cave.
He was the first near the-- the entrance on the grotto.
He runs in.
He says, Germans are here.
At the cave.
At the cave.
Germans are here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Soon, I hear Germans.
Germans are here.
I had time to put on my pants, one boot.
And I hear already Germans-- Germans are here.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
You should get out.
So what I did, I had a-- in a place that I was cleaning up, and I put in some fresh earth like-- it was so low, about four feet.
And I packed it with-- so I pull myself, and I push myself into this hole.
And I remember I was lying on the right side, and I covered with my boot my naked feet, they shouldn't see that I am there.
And I just looked like that with one eye, and I see my mother stood up.
And she comes in the front of the Germans just like that.
Jewish-- Jewish people and Jewish woman didn't talk to the gestapo.
And she stand up.
We knew to speak German.
And says to them, [SPEAKING GERMAN].
You find us here, too?
What you are afraid?
If you stay here, that the [SPEAKING GERMAN].
That the-- that the Fuhrer-- that Hitler there is going to lose the war.
We don't mix to politics.
We just want to stay here to live through the war.
And one man-- one gestapo-- and I hear somebody else.
[SPEAKING GERMAN].
An over-- it was an [GERMAN], high officer.
Says, who's talking so loud?
They speak so loud.
Who's talking so loud?
And my mother say, we don't mix to politics.
We want to just live through.
People don't live in here.
You come and you're out-- people don't live in caves.
You come out.
You're going to [GERMAN].
You're going to camp.
My mother tells him, we know what-- what kind of camps you have for us.
Just like that.
And me, I don't-- I passed out there.
That's all.
I didn't-- didn't remember nothing till I find myself next day in another place.
And I say, what happened there in the cave?
What you're telling us now is what you were told after?
When you said that-- that you passed out, and you don't know what happened--
No, I'm going to-- I'm going to come to this here.
So there's-- they took my-- my-- the-- the Germans-- we had also a cousin there.
My first cousin.
He lived another city.
He was a very nice guy and a rich guy.
His name was [POLISH].
They took him.
They took my-- my sister.
Older sister, Henia.
With Simha, with the little girl.
They took my mother's sister with the little boy.
And my mother and my younger brother.
And there were six gestapo with machine guns, with flashlights, with grenade.
They were so-- with leather coats.
And-- but there you have to crawl.
And they gave my brother to hold a little lamp.
Said my mother took it away from them.
And soon, because there is not like a-- a straight, soon we have to move a little bit.
My mother just signalled my brother, and he just jumped and left, left.
He was lying down in there.
Then my mother ran away too.
I don't know how.
I ask her once.
She says, when I came to a curve, I just put-- lie down there.
And they took out my sister with her child.
My aunt with her son, [POLISH].
And my cousin.
And they brought them out, and they gave him to the Ukrainian police.
They said, kill them.
So the Ukrainian police brought them on the-- to the jail.
And soon, we find out that they are there.
My father find out that they are there.
I work with-- I talk to them.
They talk about me, what happened with me.
We went to this doctor, [POLISH], and he knew the chief for the police.
And they offered them a lot more they should let them go.
And they made with them a deal.
And they were supposed to let them go next day, like next night.
And at night-- in the same day, a woman came in, a neighbor from my aunt, my mother's sister [POLISH].
And he came in.
And she begged the chief of the police they should let her go.
She's such a good woman.
Let her go.
So the chief of the police called this Doctor [POLISH] and says, listen.
I can't et her go.
I-- I can't let her go.
I'm not going to jeopardize myself.
The gestapo gave me to kill them.
I let her go, they know that I let her go, and something.
And he says, if you-- I'll kill them all.
Just like that.
But could-- Dr.
[POLISH] told after the story, what happened.
So what happened.
They-- they took my sister with her child.
They took my mother's sister with the-- with her son.
And-- and my cousin.
And they brought them at night.
There was a Jewish cemetery in Bilcze Zlote.
They put them down.
They cover them up.
And they were shot in the air a few times.
And they went over and they killed my-- my mother's sister with her-- with her son, a little boy.
I think eight or 10 years old.
And my sister and the child and [INAUDIBLE], they-- they let it go because they want the gold and the money.
This what happen with them.
Now, with me.
I was lying there.
I don't remember how I came out from this hole.
But what-- when my mother-- my brother came back to the cave, they find me there.
And I was-- I couldn't talk.
I couldn't-- I don't remember nothing.
Just one more thing and one more thing.
They talk to me because you have to go up, open this place.
Because I was the-- the one that-- that I made this here.
Anyway, all the people were in the chimney.
And it got-- about 4, 5 o'clock, it got dark and they opened this here.
And everybody run away.
We did push out.
And my mother start to walking with me.
My brother in one place around.
Because the police was around.
The police were there.
And I was with my mother, and my mother was almost carrying me.
And the police catch the both.
And the-- my mother knew how to talk.
And she start talking to the police.
And the police want I should go back in the cave to show where we were, because they knew that we left a lot of stuff there.
My mother said, look here.
I come-- he's sick.
He can't walk.
He can't look here what happened.
You can't take him to the cave.
My mother talked to them.
And they knew my father, because he used to them.
My mother says my father-- that my husband bought for you two bicycles, that they want.
Says, yeah.
I know.
I-- I told them that I want this, and he promised me.
He has them.
He's going to bring them.
Let me go.
And he let us go.
I don't know where I-- I was supposed to be.
I don't know what happened.
They let us go.
That's really something.
And my mother start pulling me all night.
I don't remember nothing.
And it was in March.
It was mud like that.
And she carried me maybe, I would say, seven or eight kilometers.
And she came to a place.
Now here lives a Mr.
[POLISH], a good friend of ours.
He used to come to our-- to our house always.
Now she's going to leave me here, somewhere in the stable, and she's going to go back to the city where my brother and father is.
They should come to pick me up.
This house was located-- it was in Bilcze Zlote, about eight kilometers, maybe six kilometers from the cave.
My mother start knocking on the door.
In the meantime, was snowing and raining.
It was March.
My mother told them who-- that's Esther.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Didn't open.
Didn't answer.
Knock.
And she went to another place.
She went with me another place.
Came into another place.
There were people who worked also on that [GERMAN], those-- those Ukrainian people.
Came in the house.
A-- a woman let us in.
The husband said, get out of here.
Because-- and he start dressing himself.
He's not-- if you're not going out, I'll-- I'll show you what I can do.
And the other two--
Yeah.
My mother took me, and she went to another place.
At the third house, there was some people.
They were Polish people.
[POLISH].
And I worked with-- in the [GERMAN] a mile from there, there was-- where I was working.
With their son.
Oh.
Shluke.
What happened to you?
They put me in the bath.
They covered me up over because I was shaking.
They give me something to-- to drink.
I-- and I don't-- I don't remember nothing.
Next morning, I woke up.
And I see light.
And I see-- I can't talk.
I couldn't-- I couldn't-- not talking.
Not walking.
I couldn't take a spoon to my mouth.
I had some-- some shock.
I don't know what happened.
My mother was walking to-- to Korolowka to tell my father what happened, that part of the family is in-- by the police.
Part-- my brother Sam was running away with the other people somewhere.
There was the [POLISH], which he had a hard time.
He run away with them.
And I see my brother is here with my father.
They picked me up, and they put me on the wagon.
I couldn't move.
I couldn't talk.
And they brought me home.
To Korolowka, to this bunkers what we had in our place.
Because my father and my brother still had those badges.
And they had the horse.
So I was there.
And they-- we had a doctor, Dr.
Tibles, in our place.
A Jewish doctor.
So my-- my father took me at night to this doctor.
And this doctor looked at me, because he lived next door to us.
He looked at me.
He makes like that.
Just like that.
They made for you-- a nice thing they did to you.
And he was looking in the book.
The book, was looking something.
And we still had our-- our druggist, was a Jew.
They kept the druggist.
And he gave me 12 pills.
Somehow, and slowly, slowly I got better.
And I went in in the second cave to recover.
[DRINKS]
So we were there in the city.
And suddenly, it came out if one Jew they'll find in the city, he going to be shot on spot.
Can't remain here.
And this was Pesach 1943.
So my-- we knew that they going to come.
They going to come.
My brother took us all in a wagon at night.
All the family.
And he was through side roads.
He brought us not far from the forest.
There was a barn.
A barn that a-- a farmer had a barn there, and he had-- and he kept them hay, straw.
And he brought us in and he climb up on the top.
And we were hiding there.
This was Pesach 1943.
All our family.
My father was not there.
All the kids.
I was still shaking, and still-- and my brother-- and my younger brother used to go out at night and bring in some water.
And we were there for a week.
Maybe four or five days.
And we knew there's danger there, that-- that they going to find us there.
Brother is back to the town, at night to the town.
And we went in the bunkers.
Then we say it's no good.
In the meantime, my older brother Nissen start looking for places.
He went around.
Somebody told him there is a cave there, a cave there, a cave.
He came home-- nothing.
So once, they went to the forest there, that-- where the second cave is not far.
And there was a man.
He lived in this forest.
His name was [POLISH].
This was an angel.
This was not a man.
This was some-- Was he Jewish?
No.
A Polack.
But this was-- this was somebody.
He was-- like I am telling you, an angel.
Not a man.
So my father with my brother, they both came to him, said, [POLISH].
We have got where to go.
We have to go already.
We have to go to the slaughterhouse, and they going to kill us.
That's all.
But we don't want-- we want to be alive.
[LAUGHS] Maybe you have-- maybe in your forest, we can find a place, maybe we dig a hole.
You going to help us.
He says, listen.
I have here, hiding, [POLISH], his name.
[POLISH] grandson.
Which he had-- this forest belonged to him.
The field belong.
A very rich man.
His grandson, which was my close friend.
And I have an idea.
You know, he was a hunter.
He was hunting once and he was shooting a fox.
And the fox, this-- he was running, and the fox disappear in a hole.
And he came down and sees a hole there.
Says, why don't you go down there and check.
Maybe there is something inside there.
So-- [DRINKS]
So my brother came home.
And he says what [POLISH] told him.
So on the 1st of May, 1943, on a Sunday, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] me-- I-- I could walk already.
Me and my brother and this [POLISH], [POLISH], and another man neighbor, Josel Dodek, and Mendel Dodek-- five of us-- we went to see-- to explore what's there.
We came there.
We brought with us shovel and axe, ropes, candles.
And we-- now I'll to explain you the way the cave was looking from outside.
It was a little road.
And at the right side was a forest.
And it was fields hilly up all around.
And it was like a valley.
The valley was about 200 feet wide, maybe 200 feet long.
A valley.
Low.
Has to go down, down.
And as we come to the end, there was a big ravine about 40 feet deep, and maybe about 40 feet or 30 feet wide.
And you see there water is dripping down.
And big stones there.
Water dripping down.
And we had to go down there.
Somehow, we-- we were young.
We were-- didn't take us time, and we were down there.
And as we come down there, was not till the knees, water, mud.
And we look this way.
We see a hole like a fireplace.
Exactly like a fire-- round hole.
At the bottom of the ravine.
And we look in there.
But we have no flashlights to see what's inside.
We don't know if somebody's going to move in, if he's not falling down a hundred feet.
So who goes first?
My brother.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They-- I remember on a piece of wood, I think that we tied on a rope on him.
And he was going with the feet for.
And he run down.
And he says, OK, I'm standing already.
Come in.
And we all crawled in there.
We had the light.
And we find ourselves in a little cabin, a room like 10 by 10, something like that.
Stones.
And we had a big stone here.
And we thought, that's all?
We look.
We see a-- there's an opening somewhere.
And we started crawling.
This was about four feet high.
No more.
I was 21 and 1/2, 21 years.
Oh.
We were five people.
We were five people to come to explore, to see.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- and it was about four feet high, and very low.
And we started crawling.
Lie down.
You have to crawl.
We crawled about 40 feet.
Or maybe 50. 40 feet, I would say.
And we came in in a big place.
Huge.
High.
Maybe 20 feet high.
Maybe 50 feet.
You could-- with a car, you could-- but the stones, boom.
You see a big stone, and you see it came from there.
It fell on.
